name: Platform-Integrity Trust Model Transparency
consequences: Mapping and publishing MitID's dependency on Apple/Google integrity APIs and app-distribution constraints makes the supplier-concentration risk visible and measurable rather than abstract. This shifts the narrative from privacy ideology to concrete resilience engineering, making it harder for the MitID operator or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to dismiss the issue as theoretical. However, detailed architectural disclosure may trigger confidentiality objections from the incumbent operator, requiring careful negotiation and possibly public records-act pressure.
options: Commission and publish an independent technical audit of MitID's platform-integrity assumptions, app-distribution dependencies, and telemetry mediators, structured as a peer-reviewed security paper in collaboration with Danish and international cryptography researchers.

Conduct a series of formal technical interviews with MitID operators, platform representatives, and Danish financial institutions, then synthesize findings into a structured risk register published as an institutional white paper framed around continuity-of-service and supplier concentration rather than platform criticism.

File freedom-of-information requests with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator for documentation on platform API dependencies, app-store agreements, and contingency plans, then use redacted findings to populate a public technical briefing on structural vulnerabilities.
review: Publishing MitID's platform dependencies creates political and technical visibility, but none of the options address how to extract sensitive architectural details without triggering confidentiality walls or creating legal friction with incumbent operators. The options assume disclosure is primarily a matter of persuasion and framing, but do not account for contractual or regulatory barriers that may prevent the MitID operator from sharing even high-level dependency data.

name: AltID Procurement Intervention Timing
consequences: The eIDAS2 wallet rollout (AltID) is the critical intervention point—once its architecture, API contracts, and app-distribution requirements are locked in, changing them becomes exponentially harder. Early engagement with procurement stakeholders and policy makers during the design phase can shape requirements before vendors bid and incumbent dependencies calcify. The risk is that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may view platform-neutral demands as scope creep or cost inflation, especially if Apple and Google actively lobby against restrictions.
options: Establish a formal technical advisory role within the AltID procurement process by proposing a Danish digital-identity resilience working group that reports directly to the procurement authority, with explicit remit to review platform dependencies and fallback-authentication architecture before the RFP is finalized.

Develop and circulate a model procurement clause (co-authored with legal experts and EU eIDAS2 specialists) that explicitly requires bidders to demonstrate platform-neutral access paths and fallback mechanisms, then lobby relevant procurement officials and sympathetic Folketinget members to adopt language in the official AltID requirements.

Coordinate with the EU's Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups to ensure that Danish platform-neutrality concerns are escalated as EU-level interoperability and resilience requirements, then use EU-level pressure to shape Danish procurement expectations and vendor proposals.
review: All three options assume that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen remains open to requirements changes or that EU-level pressure translates quickly into Danish procurement action. None address the scenario in which the AltID RFP is already drafted or awarded before the project's coalition and demonstrators are credible enough to influence procurement. If AltID procurement timelines are compressed or vendors are already selected, this lever becomes reactive rather than preventive.

name: Coalition Credibility and Stockholder Pressure
consequences: Building a visible coalition spanning academic researchers, civil-society privacy advocates, contingency-planning authorities, and sympathetic Folketinget members creates political and institutional pressure that individual technical work cannot. Each stakeholder group (academia, civil society, government resilience planners) brings different credibility sources and decision-making leverage. However, coalition members may have conflicting priorities—privacy advocates may focus on surveillance while resilience planners prioritize continuity—and maintaining alignment requires continuous communication and managed expectations.
options: Convene a formal multi-stakeholder steering committee including representatives from the Danish Data Protection Authority, the Department of Resilience and Contingency Planning, at least two major universities, and three civil-society organizations (digital rights, privacy, and open-standards groups), with quarterly public meetings and published minutes that frame platform neutrality as a shared institutional priority.

Orchestrate a series of op-eds, parliamentary questions, and formal submissions from coalition members (individually authored to preserve independence) that collectively establish platform-neutral Danish identity as a recurring policy concern, with explicit links to European digital-sovereignty discourse and supply-chain resilience frameworks.

Create a formal alliance between Danish academic researchers and a recognized international digital-identity research network (such as academic partners at MIT, Delft, or ETH Zurich), then publish joint research outputs and white papers that position Danish work within a broader European or transatlantic platform-neutrality movement, raising the profile and perceived expertise of the local coalition.
review: Coalition visibility creates political pressure, but none of the options address the structural incentive misalignment: the MitID operator benefits from the current Apple/Google arrangement and has little motivation to support platform-neutral alternatives. Similarly, civil-society groups may lack institutional power to influence procurement directly, and academic credibility does not automatically translate into procurement decision-making authority. The options assume that published pressure and visibility alone will shift institutional behavior.

name: Fallback-Authentication Architecture Choice
consequences: The fallback mechanism defines whether Denmark's identity system can function without Apple or Google. A browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback reduces mobile OS dependence but may lower usability for some citizens; a privacy-respecting Android fork (GrapheneOS) keeps the mobile paradigm but depends on a smaller, less-tested platform ecosystem. The choice determines both technical feasibility and political acceptance: government stakeholders may resist architectures perceived as fringe or inconvenient, while incumbent platforms will lobby for fallback requirements to remain weak or circumscribed.
options: Design and demonstrate a hardware-token-based fallback path (smartcard, FIDO2 hardware key, or NFC-based device) coupled with a web-browser authentication flow that eliminates dependency on any proprietary mobile OS, prioritizing portability and institutional control even if it reduces household citizen adoption compared to a smartphone-native approach.

Develop the primary fallback demonstrator on GrapheneOS or a comparable de-Googled Android distribution, proving that secure, standards-based authentication is feasible without reliance on Google Play Services or Apple's integrity framework, with the understanding that GrapheneOS adoption among the general population remains low but the proof-of-concept value is high.

Create a hybrid fallback path that supports both a hardware-token option (for institutional users and those seeking maximum platform independence) and a browser-based WebAuthn flow (for broader citizen accessibility), then document the architectural trade-offs and implementation costs so procurement and policy makers can make an informed choice between universality and independence.
review: Each fallback option trades off citizen accessibility against platform independence. Hardware tokens are maximally independent but impose friction on casual users; GrapheneOS demonstrates technical feasibility but is perceived as niche or activist-oriented, potentially delegitimizing the entire project in the eyes of mainstream institutional stakeholders. None of the options address how to convince ordinary citizens to adopt a fallback path they perceive as less convenient than their existing Apple or Google default, a political and behavioral problem that technical elegance does not solve.

name: External Security Review and Certification Credibility
consequences: MitID is certification-heavy; any alternative authentication path will face demands for independent security review and formal assurance. Securing credible third-party security audits and, if feasible, preliminary eIDAS or NSIS certification assessment legitimizes the demonstrators and makes it harder for skeptics to dismiss the work as unvetted research. However, formal certification is expensive, time-consuming, and requires engagement with conservative regulatory bodies that may impose requirements that constrain architectural flexibility or increase scope.
options: Commission two independent security audits (one by a recognized Danish or Nordic security firm, one by a reputable international firm) of the fallback demonstrators, structured as formal technical reports suitable for regulatory review, with explicit alignment to eIDAS "Substantial" assurance levels and NSIS requirements.

Engage directly with the Danish Data Protection Authority and the body responsible for eIDAS implementation (likely within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen) to conduct a preliminary architectural review of the fallback demonstrators, framed as informal guidance-seeking rather than formal certification application, to identify regulatory bottlenecks early and establish a pathway for future formal assurance.

Partner with a recognized European digital-identity standards body or research institute (such as ENISA, a national cybersecurity agency, or an eIDAS2-accredited technical working group) to conduct a peer-reviewed assessment of the demonstrators against European interoperability and resilience standards, using the external prestige to offset concerns about lack of formal Danish certification.
review: All three options rely on the assumption that credible external review will move institutional decision-makers, but they do not address the conflict of interest inherent in the review process: the MitID operator and incumbent platform vendors have every incentive to lobby security reviewers or regulatory bodies to impose requirements that only they can meet. Additionally, pursuing formal eIDAS certification may consume so much time and budget that the window for influencing AltID procurement closes before certification is achieved.

name: Standards and Interoperability Framing for Vendor Lock-In Prevention
consequences: Reframing the platform-neutrality issue as a European standards and interoperability problem (rather than Danish ideology) makes it harder to dismiss and aligns the project with official EU digital-sovereignty and resilience narratives. Engaging with EU eIDAS2 working groups, ENISA, and Member State coordination bodies shifts the pressure from Denmark alone to a European consensus. However, EU-level process timelines are slow, and consensus-building across 27 Member States dilutes specific pressure on Danish procurement unless the project can link European standards language directly to AltID requirements.
options: Prepare and submit formal technical input to the European Digital Identity Wallet architecture working group and any relevant ETSI or standardization bodies, focused on platform-neutral authentication APIs and certified fallback mechanisms as European-level interoperability and resilience requirements, with explicit positioning as supporting eIDAS2 implementation across the Union.

Coordinate with other Nordic and Baltic Member States (Sweden, Finland, Estonia) to develop a joint position on platform-neutral digital identity that can be presented in EU working groups and national procurement coordination forums, leveraging collective negotiating power and existing Nordic digital-governance alignment.

Commission a comparative analysis of digital-identity architecture across EU Member States, identifying which countries have implemented or are planning platform-neutral or fallback mechanisms, then publish the analysis to establish platform neutrality as an emerging European best practice rather than a Danish outlier position.
review: EU-level standards engagement is slow and diffuse; individual Member States may resist requirements perceived as increasing vendor costs or complexity. The options assume that European coordination translates into domestic Danish procurement changes, but procurement authority officials may view EU input as advisory rather than binding, especially if it conflicts with vendor proposals or budget constraints already agreed with incumbent operators.

name: Financing and De-Scoping Strategy Under Institutional Resistance
consequences: If AltID procurement is already substantially advanced or if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen signals weak buy-in by month 16, continuing to pursue direct Danish procurement influence becomes a sunk cost. The project must have a credible de-scoping pathway that preserves value by shifting to publication, EU-level standards input, and coalition consolidation. This requires accepting that direct influence on AltID may not materialize while still delivering institutional legitimacy and intellectual property that shapes future identity-infrastructure decisions. However, de-scoping may damage the project's credibility if framed as failure rather than strategic pivot.
options: Establish explicit decision gates at month 12 and month 16 tied to measurable milestones (published feasibility report, formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator, demonstrators with documented security review), with pre-agreed de-scope criteria that shift phase three from AltID procurement pressure toward publication, EU-standards input, and open-source artifact release if Danish procurement channels close.

Build a contingency funding reserve (within the 3.5 million DKK phase three budget) explicitly allocated to extended publication, international research collaboration, and EU-standards engagement, with the understanding that this reserve is deployed if direct AltID influence proves infeasible, ensuring the project delivers knowledge and standards input regardless of domestic procurement outcomes.

Negotiate a secondary partnership with a Nordic university or research consortium to adopt the project's demonstrators, intellectual property, and coalition as a foundation for a longer-term Horizon Europe or Nordic Research Council initiative focused on European platform-neutral digital identity, creating an institutional home for the work if the Danish domestic window closes.
review: De-scoping or pivoting to EU-level work requires accepting that the project's primary objective—influencing AltID procurement—may not be achieved. The options attempt to preserve credibility and long-term impact through secondary channels, but they do not address the political and institutional difficulty of publicly de-committing from an original objective without appearing to have failed. Financing contingency reserves or securing follow-on funding from international sources is also uncertain and may not materialize if the initial Danish effort is perceived as unsuccessful.

name: Resilience Framing and Contingency-Risk Narrative
consequences: Reframing the platform-dependency issue as a critical resilience and continuity-of-service problem—rather than privacy ideology—shifts institutional attention from abstract sovereignty concerns toward concrete failure scenarios (e.g., app-store policy changes, geopolitical sanctions, or API discontinuation affecting public authentication). This narrative resonates with contingency-planning stakeholders and procurement officers who manage supplier risk. The trade-off is that purely privacy-focused or anti-corporate advocates may view this framing as diluting their core message, and resilience language alone does not guarantee procurement action if incumbents frame the status quo as already resilient.
options: Commission and publish a formal continuity-of-service risk assessment quantifying the specific operational impact of a hypothetical year-long app-store distribution suspension or platform API deprecation on critical Danish public services, using real examples from other jurisdictions where similar disruptions occurred.

Partner with Denmark's national emergency-management and civil-contingency authorities to integrate platform-dependency analysis into official resilience and critical-infrastructure planning documents, positioning the fallback-authentication requirement as a national-continuity obligation rather than a digital-services preference.

Develop a scenario-based procurement briefing for the Danish State Concessions Board that models total-cost-of-ownership and service-continuity implications of single-platform-dependent versus multi-pathway authentication models over a ten-year horizon, translated into language familiar to budget and risk-management officials.
review: The resilience frame effectively depoliticizes the sovereignty argument and creates institutional cover for procurement intervention, but none of the options explicitly address the counter-narrative incumbents will deploy: that Apple and Google themselves have stated commitment to serving critical infrastructure and that their platforms already include fallback mechanisms (web-based access, SMS backup). The options must anticipate and neutralize the claim that the status quo is already resilient enough.

name: Demonstrator Scope and Visibility Calibration
consequences: The technical demonstrators are not production systems and must remain credible without overselling feasibility. Calibrating their scope and public visibility determines whether they function as evidence of technical viability or as ammunition for incumbents to dismiss the work as infeasible research. Lean demonstrators—clearly scoped to FIDO2/WebAuthn and GrapheneOS—build credibility with technical and regulatory audiences. Over-ambitious scope or claims of near-production readiness invite both security criticism and accusations of naive underestimation of deployment complexity.
options: Maintain strict demonstrator scope focused on authentication-protocol feasibility and fallback-path architecture using open standards, but publish detailed technical documentation and conduct a credible external security review by a respected third party, positioning the work as peer-reviewed evidence rather than product-development proof-of-concept.

Release the demonstrators as minimal viable prototypes in a public repository with explicit warnings about non-production status and architectural simplifications, inviting academic and independent security review while avoiding any language suggesting imminent deployment or competitive parity with MitID.

Restrict public access to the demonstrators until they have been formally reviewed by a Danish public-sector security authority or eIDAS-qualified auditor, then publish a sanitized technical summary and certified-findings document that emphasizes validation and third-party verification over raw code or feature completeness.
review: Demonstrator credibility hinges on managing institutional expectations: too much transparency risks enabling incumbent security critiques, while too much secrecy fuels skepticism about actual feasibility. The options focus on third-party validation but do not address the risk that even well-reviewed demonstrators will be dismissed as 'toy systems' that ignore operational complexity (e.g., revocation, credential issuance workflows, cross-sector interoperability) that real identity systems must solve.

name: Coalition Orchestration and Bloc Pressure Sequencing
consequences: Building a coalition spanning civil-society, academic, security, and contingency-planning stakeholders creates institutional pressure on procurement and standards processes, but coalition credibility depends on strategic sequencing of public endorsements and behind-the-scenes institutional engagement. Premature public coalition announcements risk alerting incumbents to align counter-pressure or co-opt sympathetic voices; too much back-channel engagement delays visible momentum and allows institutional inertia to persist. Coalition composition also signals priorities—a privacy-dominated coalition is easier to dismiss as ideological, while a resilience-and-continuity coalition is harder to marginalize.
options: Conduct confidential coalition building during phase one with government-adjacent bodies (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, State Concessions Board, civil-contingency authorities) and academic institutions, deferring public endorsement announcements until phase three after demonstrators and formal proposals are available, maximizing credibility and institutional readiness when pressure is applied.

Orchestrate a series of carefully sequenced public interventions—starting with published academic research and security-community position papers during phase one, escalating to civil-society joint letters and parliamentary committee testimony during phase two, and culminating in formal coalition endorsements only after demonstrators exist—creating cumulative pressure without alerting incumbents prematurely.

Build a narrowly credentialed technical advisory council of recognized international security and identity experts who publicly validate the feasibility report and demonstrators, complemented by a separate non-public coalition of institutional stakeholders who provide behind-the-scenes procurement and standards leverage without visible coordination.
review: Coalition pressure is most effective when sequenced to align with procurement decision-gates and standards-development timelines, but none of the options explicitly model the timing of AltID procurement decisions or EU wallet-framework standards cycles. Without coordinating coalition pressure to these external gates, the project risks building momentum that dissipates before institutional decision points arrive.

name: Standards Leverage and EU Alignment Strategy
consequences: Engaging EU bodies and the European Digital Identity Wallet framework creates multilevel pressure: national procurement authorities want alignment with EU standards to avoid fragmentation, while EU bodies are genuinely concerned with interoperability and platform lock-in. However, Danish influence on EU-level standards is limited, and EU wallet frameworks move on multi-year cycles that may not synchronize with AltID timelines. Timing and venue matter—early engagement can shape emerging standards; late engagement locks the project into commenting on already-drafted language with limited revision capacity.
options: Initiate direct engagement with the European Telecommunication Standards Institute and relevant eIDAS2 working groups during phase one, focusing on contributing to draft standards language on platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms, positioning Denmark as a standards-leadership voice rather than a unilateral outlier.

Coordinate with other EU member states (e.g., Germany, Netherlands, Sweden) that share platform-dependency concerns to submit joint comments and position papers to EU working groups and the Commission, amplifying Denmark's voice through multi-national coalition and shared regulatory interest.

Delay EU-level engagement until phase three after demonstrators and procurement influence are visible, then present the Danish case as a real-world model for platform-neutral and fallback-capable identity architectures, using it as evidence for EU-level standards requirements rather than proposing hypothetical designs.
review: EU alignment is a powerful tool if timed correctly, but none of the options address the fundamental misalignment between EU standards-development cycles (typically 2–3 year horizon) and this project's 24-month timeline. If AltID procurement decisions harden before EU standards shift, EU-level advocacy becomes retrospective validation rather than prospective intervention.

name: Financing Conditionality and De-Scoping Governance
consequences: Phase two funding is explicitly conditional on phase one producing specific institutional engagement and a documented financing plan. However, the project also includes a de-scoping provision for phase three if formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16. This dual conditionality creates tension: strict enforcement protects against sunk costs and wasted work on procurement influence with no institutional reception, but premature de-scoping abandons leverage just as institutional timelines are converging. Financing uncertainty also creates risk of team dispersal and loss of momentum.
options: Establish a formal gating review at month 15 conducted by an independent panel of government, academic, and security stakeholders who assess institutional traction based on explicit metrics (documented meetings, formal responses, inclusion in official documents), then transparently decide phase three scope—either full procurement-intervention mode or consolidation-and-publication-only mode—with financing committed for either path.

Secure phase three financing (3.5 million DKK) upfront as commitment funding contingent only on phase one completion and delivery of demonstrators, removing financing uncertainty and enabling the project to sustain engagement with AltID even if formal institutional traction appears weak, with clear success metrics defined in advance rather than assessed retrospectively.

Negotiate a tiered phase three budget with lower baseline funding (1.5 million DKK) for publication and coalition consolidation activities, plus conditional additional funding (up to 2.0 million) released only if institutional engagement benchmarks are met by month 16, balancing risk management with flexibility to pursue high-impact procurement and standards opportunities.
review: Financing conditionality creates accountability and protects against sunk costs, but conditional gating introduces timing risk: if the gating review at month 15 occurs just as AltID procurement processes are entering decision phases (month 16–18), the project may lack adequate runway to influence procurement outcomes. The options focus on financial governance but do not explicitly synchronize conditionality gates with AltID procurement timelines.

name: Incumbent Deflection and Co-Option Mitigation
consequences: Apple and Google will likely respond to the project's pressure through a combination of deflection (claiming existing fallback mechanisms are sufficient), engagement (co-opting concerns into minor product feature announcements), and counter-advocacy (partnering with incumbents in Danish digital services to frame platform-neutral requirements as operationally infeasible). The project must anticipate and neutralize these strategies without directly attacking incumbents in ways that trigger defensive institutional alignment or legal counter-measures. Credible technical evidence and institutional framing matter more than rhetorical assault.
options: Publish an independent technical audit of Apple and Google's existing fallback and platform-neutral access mechanisms (e.g., web-based authentication, SMS backup, web-push APIs) comparing their operational availability, security properties, and user-experience friction to proposed open-standards fallbacks, allowing the evidence to contradict incumbent claims without adversarial framing.

Engage diplomatically with Apple and Google during phase one and two, presenting the project as a resilience and interoperability conversation rather than an attack, and explore whether they would support (or at minimum not oppose) procurement language requiring documented fallback mechanisms and third-party certification—converting potential opponents into neutral or tacit allies.

Avoid direct technical comparison to incumbents' systems; instead, focus messaging on affirmative benefits of open standards, interoperability, and regulatory resilience, leaving it to institutional stakeholders and the media to note gaps in incumbent offerings without the project appearing to single out specific vendors.
review: Incumbent deflection is likely regardless of project framing, and the options focus on evidence and diplomacy, which are sound but incomplete. None of the options address the risk that incumbents will offer narrow, cosmetic concessions (e.g., a minor fallback feature) just enough to blunt institutional pressure and procurement action—transforming the project's influence into marginal product improvements rather than structural change.

name: Political Window and Parliamentary Advocacy Timing
consequences: Denmark's Folketinget digital-policy committee and relevant parliamentary actors are sympathetic to sovereignty and resilience framing but are not natural constituencies for deep technical identity policy. Parliamentary interest is tied to broader digital-governance narratives (data protection, EU alignment, critical infrastructure) and is subject to electoral and policy cycles. Mobilizing parliamentary support at the right moment can lock institutional commitment and procurement requirements into formal policy, but mistiming or over-reliance on individual champions creates fragility if parliamentary focus shifts or champions lose influence.
options: Develop a formal policy proposal and white paper for submission to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and Folketinget during phase one, timed to coincide with scheduled parliamentary digital-governance reviews or when related identity or data-protection legislation is under debate, maximizing visibility and institutional momentum.

Cultivate relationships with sympathetic members of the digital-policy committee during phase one, positioning the project as an independent evidence resource rather than advocacy, and invite parliamentary testimony or engagement only when demonstrators and institutional support are credible—delaying formal parliamentary push until phase three to maximize impact when evidence is available.

Coordinate public outreach and media engagement timed to align with announcement of AltID procurement timelines or EU Digital Identity Wallet developments, creating external urgency and parliamentary attention without explicitly lobbying individual members or over-committing to a specific political alignment.
review: Parliamentary engagement is valuable for locking institutional commitment, but none of the options address the core risk: parliamentary interest in identity policy is sporadic and often reactive to crises or EU requirements. If the project's evidence and advocacy reach parliament during a period of lower legislative focus on digital identity, the impact may be diluted regardless of project quality. The options assume parliamentary receptivity but do not specify how the project will monitor and capitalize on windows of parliamentary attention.

name: Scope Ambiguity and Strategic Optionality Preservation
consequences: The project is framed as a feasibility and advocacy effort focused on AltID influence, but deliberately preserving ambiguity about whether the ultimate goal is procurement requirements, technical standards, bilateral engagement with incumbents, or simply ensuring the issue remains on institutional agendas prevents early co-option or dismissal by stakeholders uncomfortable with any single outcome. However, strategic ambiguity also creates internal coherence risks: the team may pursue incompatible objectives, and institutional partners may misunderstand or misalign with actual goals, leading to wasted effort or failed engagement.
options: Maintain explicit scope flexibility in all institutional communications and formal documents, stating that the project's objectives span procurement influence, standards development, incumbent engagement, and contingency planning, with prioritization determined by institutional receptivity and timeline convergence—allowing the project to pivot toward the highest-impact lever as conditions reveal themselves.

Define a primary objective (e.g., procurement requirement inclusion in AltID) with secondary objectives (standards influence, incumbent engagement) framed as complementary, communicating this hierarchy transparently to institutional partners while preserving internal flexibility to shift resources if the primary path encounters insurmountable friction.

Explicitly separate the project's intellectual contributions (feasibility reports, demonstrators, standards input) from institutional advocacy objectives, positioning the technical work as independent research output with multiple potential institutional applications, rather than tying the project's success to a single procurement or standards outcome.
review: Strategic ambiguity preserves flexibility but creates coherence and accountability risks. None of the options address how the project will measure success if the chosen lever shifts mid-course: if the project pivots from procurement focus to standards input to incumbent engagement, the initial success criteria become meaningless, and it becomes difficult to assess whether the project has truly influenced institutional outcomes or merely scattered effort across multiple weak pressures.

name: Fallback-Access Certification and Trust-Model Portability
consequences: Establishing a formal certification pathway for platform-neutral fallback authentication creates regulatory legitimacy without requiring wholesale MitID replacement. This shifts the burden of proof from the project (proving equivalence to Apple/Google signals) to the incumbent operators (proving why fallback paths remain too risky for essential services). However, certification requirements themselves may become new bottlenecks if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen demands the same rigorous review timelines as MitID, effectively freezing alternative pathways in time.
options: Develop a lightweight eIDAS2 Substantial-level certification framework specifically for browser-based or hardware-token fallback mechanisms, targeting completion by month 20 to inform AltID procurement language.

Propose a time-limited pilot certification (24-month renewable) for platform-neutral authentication in lower-risk public services (e.g., healthcare portal access, tax information retrieval) to accumulate operational evidence without full production rollout.

Embed fallback-authentication trust requirements into the existing MitID operator's contract renewal negotiation, framing platform-neutral backup as a compliance obligation rather than a competitive alternative.
review: The core tension is that certification itself becomes gatekeeping if authorities require the same evidence burden for fallback paths as they do for primary systems. All three options assume certification bodies will move faster than they historically have; none adequately addresses what happens if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen treats fallback certification as a lower priority compared to AltID's own eIDAS2 compliance schedule.

name: Demonstrator Realism and De-Risking Perception
consequences: Credible but intentionally limited demonstrators (non-production, simulated environment, no live MitID integration) can prove technical feasibility without triggering incumbent concerns about unauthorized system access or premature competition. The risk is that stakeholders dismiss demonstrators as toys if they are too spartan, or as threats if they appear too complete and functional. Calibration directly affects whether phase two funding is released and whether phase three procurement engagement is taken seriously.
options: Build a fully functional mobile demonstrator on GrapheneOS with realistic FIDO2/WebAuthn integration against a simulated public-identity-provider environment, published as open-source code with independent security audit, to maximize credibility with technical audiences and procurement teams.

Construct a deliberately minimal browser-based proof-of-concept (HTML/JavaScript, no native mobile code) that demonstrates the cryptographic plausibility of fallback authentication without claiming production readiness, reducing incumbent perception of competitive threat.

Commission a published technical report analyzing three historical fallback-authentication case studies from other European countries (Estonia, France, Netherlands) paired with a lightweight reference implementation showing how each pattern could apply to Danish eIDAS2, avoiding direct MitID simulation entirely.
review: The core tension is demonstrator scope: too complete and it looks like you are building a replacement; too minimal and it appears academic and disconnected from real deployment constraints. All options assume you can control how stakeholders interpret the demonstrator's intent; none account for the incumbent operator's incentive to reframe even a published reference implementation as a threat to certified integrity and liability frameworks.

name: Continuity-Risk Narrative Ownership and Crisis-Scenario Framing
consequences: Reframing the issue as continuity planning, supplier concentration risk, and geopolitical contingency (rather than privacy or vendor criticism) creates political and administrative buy-in from risk-management and resilience stakeholders who would otherwise dismiss the project as ideology. This narrative is harder for incumbents to dismiss as anti-business. However, overplaying the crisis angle risks appearing alarmist and inviting backlash that the project lacks concrete evidence of imminent failure.
options: Develop a formal continuity-of-service white paper co-authored with Danish emergency-preparedness authorities and published under resilience or civil-protection framing, explicitly modeling scenarios where Apple or Google policy changes, supply-chain disruptions, or geopolitical sanctions affect access to essential public services.

Commission an independent risk assessment from a recognized international security consultancy (e.g., Mandiant, iSight Partners) that documents supplier-concentration vulnerabilities in MitID and ranks platform-neutral fallback as a risk-mitigation priority, lending third-party credibility to the framing.

Frame phase three engagement with AltID as part of Denmark's mandatory critical-infrastructure resilience planning under NIS2 Directive obligations, positioning platform-neutral access as a cybersecurity and supply-chain-resilience requirement rather than a feature request.
review: The core tension is that continuity-risk framing is politically powerful but empirically thin: there is no documented incident of Apple or Google blocking Danish authentication, only structural vulnerability. All three options rely on authorities accepting the *possibility* of crisis; none explain what concrete trigger or decision point should activate fallback pathways, creating a moral-hazard problem where the fallback system may never actually be used and thus never validated.

name: Coalition Composition and Incumbent Co-Option Resistance
consequences: Building a diverse coalition spanning civil society, academic researchers, and digital-sovereignty advocates creates political weight and technical credibility. However, if the coalition includes sympathetic figures from within the MitID operator or telecommunications incumbents, they gain influence over project messaging and may soften platform-neutrality demands in exchange for participation. Conversely, an aggressively anti-incumbent coalition risks being dismissed as ideological and may lose access to decision-makers who fear reputational association.
options: Construct a transparent multi-stakeholder coalition with formal governance (published membership, open meetings, documented positions) including privacy advocates, academic security researchers, consumer-protection bodies, and digital-rights organizations, explicitly excluding vendor representatives and maintaining independence from incumbent operators.

Invite selective participation from within the MitID ecosystem (e.g., a sympathetic bank or public-sector IT department) as coalition members with clearly delineated roles, so that inside perspectives inform the project without compromising external credibility or platform-neutral messaging.

Build a narrower coalition focused exclusively on academic, regulatory, and EU-level partners (university researchers, ENISA, European Digital Rights, relevant member-state authorities), reducing domestic incumbent influence while positioning the project as aligned with pan-European digital sovereignty rather than Danish vendor criticism.
review: The core tension is that inclusion breeds credibility but risks cooptation, while exclusion maintains purity but reduces institutional access. All three options assume the coalition's composition directly controls stakeholder perception; none addresses the likelihood that incumbents will lobby coalition members privately, offering commercial partnerships or funding that create exit incentives before critical procurement moments arrive.

name: EU Regulatory Alignment and Standards Pressure Sequencing
consequences: Aligning Danish advocacy with broader European Digital Identity Wallet framework discussions creates regulatory momentum and legitimacy that individual member states struggle to generate alone. Standards pressure from EU bodies can reframe platform-neutrality as a regulatory compliance issue rather than a Danish preference. The trade-off is that EU-level processes move slowly and are heavily influenced by incumbent vendors; Danish input can be diluted in multilateral discussions and the project risks becoming trapped in technical working groups with no procurement outcome.
options: Engage directly with the European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and ENISA consultation processes, submitting position papers on fallback-authentication requirements and platform-neutrality language by month 14, with the goal of influencing the wallet framework's final guidance before AltID procurement specifications are locked in.

Coordinate with sympathetic EU member states (Estonia, France, Netherlands) known for digital-sovereignty concerns to co-author and publish a joint policy statement on platform-neutral access requirements, amplifying Danish concerns through multilateral pressure rather than unilateral advocacy.

Propose a formal amendment or clarification to the eIDAS2 implementing acts requesting explicit language on platform-neutrality, fallback mechanisms, and supplier-concentration risk mitigation, submitted through Denmark's permanent representation to the European Commission and relevant Council working groups.
review: The core tension is that EU-level influence is legitimizing but slow, and formal amendments to eIDAS2 are unlikely to be granted mid-implementation. All three options assume the European Digital Identity Wallet framework is genuinely open to input and has not already been captured by incumbent vendor interests; none addresses the risk that Apple and Google's existing relationships with European regulators and consumer-focused framing may outweigh digital-sovereignty arguments in final guidance documents.

name: AltID Procurement Language and Contractual Lock-In Prevention
consequences: Inserting platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into AltID's formal procurement specification or framework agreement creates binding obligations that cannot be easily reversed after vendor selection. This is the most direct lever for institutional change. However, if AltID procurement is already advanced by the time the project matures (likely by month 18–20), this lever becomes ineffective; procurement language inserted late is often treated as non-essential or deferred to future phases that may never materialize.
options: Produce a detailed procurement language template (targeting Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's technical requirements document) by month 15 that explicitly mandates platform-neutral access capabilities and includes weighted scoring criteria favoring fallback-authentication implementations, submitted through formal consultation channels before AltID vendor solicitation is published.

Propose that AltID's framework agreement include a mandatory post-launch review (month 30) assessing platform-neutrality compliance and triggering contingency-access pathway activation if Apple or Google policy changes affect digital-identity availability, creating contractual accountability for platform-neutral design.

Coordinate with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to embed a formal platform-neutrality pilot obligation into AltID's initial scope, allocating budget and timeline for a 12-month pilot fallback-authentication service running in parallel with primary MitID/AltID systems during months 24–36.
review: The core tension is timing: procurement language is only powerful if injected before vendor solicitation, and AltID's timeline may not align with the project's phase-completion milestones. All three options assume Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will actively incorporate unsolicited procurement language; none accounts for the bureaucratic reality that procurement specialists often ignore external input once internal specifications are drafted, treating late suggestions as scope creep or vendor favoritism.

name: Workforce Capability Concentration and Organizational Dependency Risk
consequences: Building the project's technical and policy teams with skill redundancy and documented knowledge transfer prevents the project from becoming dependent on a single researcher or specialist, which would make it fragile and less credible to institutional partners. Investing in team capacity also signals to stakeholders that the project is serious and durable. The trade-off is that additional hiring increases budget pressure and introduces coordination overhead; a leaner team may move faster initially but becomes a single point of failure if key personnel depart or are unavailable during critical phases.
options: Hire two parallel technical leads (one mobile/platform-focused, one standards/browser-based-authentication-focused) from month 1, with explicit knowledge-sharing protocols and cross-training on all major deliverables, ensuring no single engineer is irreplaceable.

Structure the project to rotate policy and research responsibilities across two part-time researchers (shared lead position) rather than one full-time researcher, creating redundancy and preventing burnout while requiring formalized handoff and documentation discipline.

Establish formal advisory roles for three external subject-matter experts (one mobile security researcher, one digital-identity procurement specialist, one European regulatory affairs consultant) contracted for specific milestones (end of phase 1, end of phase 2, end of phase 3) to validate work quality and create institutional memory outside the core team.
review: The core tension is cost versus resilience: team redundancy protects the project against unexpected departures or capacity bottlenecks but consumes budget that might otherwise fund technical work or external credibility-building. All three options assume budget is sufficient to support the additional capacity; none confronts the political reality that if institutional traction is weak by month 12, budget will be cut and the larger team will be seen as overhead rather than insurance.
